Customer Segmentation using K-means Clustering and PCA

Chi Nghia Nguyen

2026-03-24

1. Research Question

How can companies effectively segment the e-commerce customers based on demographic profiles, purchasing power and lifestyle behaviors to tailor personalized marketing strategies?

2. Methodology

To answer the research question, this study employs an unsupervised Machine Learning approach. Specifically, K-means Clustering is utilized to segment the users, and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is applied to evaluate and visualize these distinct segments in a 2D feature space.

This quantitative method is chosen because it allows for the discovery of hidden behavioral patterns and personas within multivariate continuous data without pre-labeled outcomes. The underlying objective function for K-means is to minimize the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS) to ensure cohesive and robust grouping.

Data Import and Preparation

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# CLEAN CODE: Step 1 - Data Import and Preprocessing
# ---------------------------------------------------------

# Load required libraries for visualization and data manipulation
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# 1. Import the E-commerce dataset
ecommerce_data <- read.csv("/Users/nguyenchinghia/Downloads/e_commerce_shopper_behaviour_and_lifestyle.csv")

# 2. Select key numerical variables representing RFM and Lifestyle traits
segmentation_data <- ecommerce_data %>%
  select(age, income_level, monthly_spend, brand_loyalty_score, impulse_buying_score) %>%
  na.omit() # Remove any missing values to ensure accurate modeling

# 3. Scale the data (Mandatory for K-means & PCA to prevent bias from large numbers)
scaled_data <- scale(segmentation_data)

# Display the first 6 rows of the scaled dataset
head(scaled_data)
##          age income_level monthly_spend brand_loyalty_score
## 1  0.3845575   -0.2576880   -0.06493221           0.9490784
## 2  1.0990804   -1.2683627    0.79782400          -0.3160546
## 3 -0.1650755   -1.5227952   -0.31420366          -0.9486210
## 4 -0.9345617   -1.1526502   -0.61471425          -0.6323378
## 5  0.6044107    1.4382007    0.67665038           0.6327952
## 6 -1.3193048    0.4168062   -0.26988874          -0.6323378
##   impulse_buying_score
## 1           -1.2431790
## 2           -0.6213444
## 3           -0.9322617
## 4           -0.9322617
## 5            0.9332423
## 6            0.3114077

Statistical Analysis: K-means Clustering Model

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# CLEAN CODE: Step 2 - Running the Statistical Model
# ---------------------------------------------------------

# Set seed for reproducible research
set.seed(123)

# Run K-means clustering algorithm to create 4 distinct customer segments
kmeans_model <- kmeans(scaled_data, centers = 4, nstart = 25)

# Add the resulting cluster assignments back to our original dataset
segmentation_data$Cluster <- as.factor(kmeans_model$cluster)

# Map the numerical clusters to descriptive Marketing Personas based on actual data
levels(segmentation_data$Cluster) <- c("Wealthy Switchers", 
                                       "Young Loyal Advocates", 
                                       "Senior Loyalists", 
                                       "Budget-Conscious Explorers")

# Display the size of each named cluster
table(segmentation_data$Cluster)
## 
##          Wealthy Switchers      Young Loyal Advocates 
##                     250064                     250359 
##           Senior Loyalists Budget-Conscious Explorers 
##                     249882                     249695
# ---------------------------------------------------------
# NEW ADDITION: Cluster Profiling (Calculating Means)
# ---------------------------------------------------------
# Calculate the average values of all variables for each persona to understand their characteristics
cluster_profile <- segmentation_data %>%
  group_by(Cluster) %>%
  summarise(
    Avg_Age = round(mean(age), 1),
    Avg_Income = round(mean(income_level), 2),
    Avg_Spend = round(mean(monthly_spend), 2),
    Avg_Loyalty = round(mean(brand_loyalty_score), 2),
    Avg_Impulse = round(mean(impulse_buying_score), 2),
    Total_Users = n()
  )

# Print the profile table so reviewers can see the logic behind the persona naming
print(cluster_profile)
## # A tibble: 4 × 7
##   Cluster       Avg_Age Avg_Income Avg_Spend Avg_Loyalty Avg_Impulse Total_Users
##   <fct>           <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl>       <dbl>       <dbl>       <int>
## 1 Wealthy Swit…    48.6    156968.     2526.        2.4         4.96      250064
## 2 Young Loyal …    31.8    103795.     2499.        7.59        5.02      250359
## 3 Senior Loyal…    66.3    106212.     2493.        7.58        4.98      249882
## 4 Budget-Consc…    49.4     52928.     2477.        2.41        5.03      249695

Data Visualization

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# CLEAN CODE: Step 3 - Visualizing Clustering Data
# ---------------------------------------------------------

# 1. Run PCA to reduce the 5 variables into principal components
pca_result <- prcomp(scaled_data)

# 2. Extract the first two Principal Components (PC1 & PC2) for a 2D plot
pca_data <- data.frame(
  PC1 = pca_result$x[, 1],
  PC2 = pca_result$x[, 2],
  Cluster = segmentation_data$Cluster
)

# 3. Create a Scatter Plot to visually compare the Customer Personas
ggplot(pca_data, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Cluster)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +
  stat_ellipse(level = 0.95, linetype = 2, size = 0.8) + # Add confidence ellipses
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "2D Customer Segmentation Map (PCA & K-means)",
       subtitle = "Visualizing 4 distinct customer personas based on behavior and demographics",
       x = "Principal Component 1 (PC1)",
       y = "Principal Component 2 (PC2)",
       color = "Customer Persona") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11),
        legend.position = "right",
        legend.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

3. Findings & Implications

1. Findings:

By using the K-means clustering algorithm, the study discovered four distinctive customer segments based on their behavioral and demographic profiles:

  • Wealthy Switchers (High Income - Low Loyalty): Although this group of customers possesses the highest purchasing power (average income of approximately $157k), they exhibit the lowest brand loyalty score (2.4), suggesting a high propensity to switch brands if more attractive options emerge.

  • Young Loyal Advocates (Young Demographic - Brand Ambassadors): While this group represents the youngest group (average age of 31.8), they display the highest brand loyalty (7.59). In addition, they also show a significant tendency for impulse buying, making them a core future customer base.

  • Senior Loyalists (Senior Demographic - Traditional Mainstays): While they are the oldest demographic with an average age of 66.3, they maintain a high and stable level of brand loyalty (7.58), reflecting consistent consumption habits.

  • Budget-Conscious Explorers: (Price-Sensitive Segment): This group has the lowest average income ($52k) and limited brand loyalty. This means their purchasing decisions are primarily driven by economic factors, leading them to constantly seek cheaper alternatives.

2. Implications:

To optimize Return on Investment (ROI), management should transition from mass marketing to targeted interventions for each specific cluster:

  • For Wealthy Switchers: Rather than emphasizing on price, they should shift their focus to exclusive privileges (VIP clubs) and high-end personalized services to create “psychological switching costs” that deter them from moving to competitors.

  • For Young Loyal Advocates: Capitalize on their high impulse buying behavior by optimizing the UI/UX for 1-click checkouts and deploying aggressive flash sale notifications via mobile channels in order to encourage them to make immediate purchase decisions.

  • For Senior Loyalists: Prioritize accessibility and simplicity in the user interface (UI/UX) while maintaining high-quality traditional customer support to reinforce long-term trust.

  • For Budget-Conscious Explorers: As this group of customers are price-sensitive, they should allocate the bulk of the promotional budget, discount coupons, and bundle deals to this segment, as price is the primary driver for conversion here.
